title: 2025/10/15 - GA Subcommittee B (1 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-10-14 - Governance and Administration Committee - Public Service Amendment Bill [Subcommittee B]
description: Wednesday, 15th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee B Public Service Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: National Delegate Team at Department of Conservation, Public Service Association Paula Warren, PSA National Delegate Policy Hub - Victoria University of Wellington Andrew Jackson, Operational Manager Dr. Malcolm Menzies Parents of Vision Impaired NZ Dr Rebekah Graham, National Executive Officer Asian Legal Network Darryn Ooi, Co-lead advocacy Office of the Clerk Dr. David Wilson, Clerk of the House of Representatives
descriptionSummary: Public Service Amendment Bill. National Delegate Team at Department of Conservation, Public Service Association. Paula Warren, PSA National Delegate

chunks.text: Um, we are going live. Um, thank you for joining us, and we are gonna jump straight into our first, uh, submission from, uh, the National Delegate Team. It says at Department of Conservation, but I assume it's for the department Co or, or this is the PSA team from the Department of Conservation.

Have we got that right? Excellent. Well, we have got 10 minutes for you. Um, I'll let you know when there's a minute to run, but the time's all yours. Thank you. Um, so perhaps if I can start by explaining the National Delegate Team. Um, so the PSA is basically the only union now operating in doc. Um, although we do, I think still have a couple of old guys who belong to a Ones our membership, which is the majority of staff elect local delegates, it's local delegates, then the national delegates who form the national Delegate team.

And our NDC meets once a month. And at the moment, because we have a lot of restructurings on, we meet more regularly and share information. The National delegate team, uh, has a formal high performance engagement relationship with management because fundamentally, our goals are much the same as the department's goals.

We want our members to be working in a highly productive workplace where they are safe and they have high wellbeing. So increasingly we end doc work together, but this is very much a submission from the delegates. The delegates look at the business from a slightly different lens.

Um, and, and this is our contribution to the debate. So in our work, one of the things that's obviously very important to people when they join DOC is the fact that they're joining a business which has very long term goals and has to run long-term programs.

And our members want to be able to be part of programs that run seamlessly with continuous improvement in how well they can operate. So there's been a lot of improvement in our ability to do offshore island eradications, pest control operations, manage these, work with our volunteers, all of that sort of stuff.

And so we are a bit concerned that the changes to the legislation, which are proposed in this bill, will remove some of that focus on that long, long-term program, long-term stewardship role that operational government agencies need to have.

Ministers don't have the time or, or the interest, I imagine to be involved in every day-to-day thing that we have to do. They expect most of the business to just keep running and to keep running well and to constantly improve. And they use legislative changes, policy changes, and the estimates to steer the ship, not to run everything about the ship.

And, and so we are concerned that that balance between the day-to-day goals of the government and that long-term public service role for the public is, is not well balanced if the changes that are proposed here are made. So that's fundamentally our concern in terms of the diversity.

We strongly support, um, appointment by Merit. That's what we see largely happening in the public service. Um, but we also want diverse teams because diversity brings a range of ways of approaching jobs. If you want to constantly innovate, you have to have some diversity.

So again, we'd be reluctant to see references to diversity lost from the legislation. We looked closely at the bill and at the core legislation, and we couldn't see that the diversity words were in any way a barrier to merit appointments and those other principles, which are very clear.

So that's our submission. Thank you. Thank you. Um, Francisco Hernandez, uh, thank you so much. She, thank you so much for, um, your submission. Um, I do wanna pick up that point around, uh, you know, merit based appointments in the public service and also what you had to say around the diversity of thought thing because, you know, the minister herself has acknowledged that, um, she hasn't seen any evidence that there's no merit based appoint, that there's a lack of merit based appointment in the public service.

Could you elaborate on what you said? Uh, how on your submission where you wrote how managers were, uh, recruiting people who are like them in the way they look and would approach the work and not appointing similarly qualified people who would approach the job differently? It's a general problem with humans. Oh, yes.

You know, so particularly if you use an interview process. So a whole lot of people walk into the interview and if they have quite similar basic skill sets, so they're all quite pointable. But, but for example, let, let me give an example, um, of the appointment of an operations manager during a restructuring some time back where a woman from who had been working at the national level wanted to be considered for the job, um, and was not, and in my view, that was partly because the recruiting manager had a very

specific picture in his head of what an ops manager looks like. And an ops manager, you know, is somebody who used to do fencing. It's not somebody who worked in policy and then in, in national issues management. So, so to me, what I was seeing was very much that lens of I have a vision of lots sort of person we want, and it's based on what we've had in the past, and it's based on what I am because I'm what we had in the past.

Um, and, and so we often end up with difficulties getting shifts in our workforce because of that, that constant maintenance of the same kind of workforce. And, and so we want to see workforce planning that actually thinks about that and thinks about what kind of people we need in future.

Um, and just one question from me. You just in your first part, and you, and you go into quite some detail in your submission, but just about the long term sort of work that Dr and I think of EY Island where, where I come from, um, a a great result, right? Fantastic work that you do out there. What, what specifically would you do you think needs to change that would otherwise, if it goes through as the bill is written now, you won't be able to sort of do that?

I am not entirely sure that we wouldn't be able to do that, but it removes the emphasis on that. And I think very much when the Public Service Commission is giving advice to Chief Executives is advising on how the overall public service system works, that they won't be prodded to think about that and to think about what what they are doing might impede that kind of program.

And, you know, if you think capi, I mean CAPI was set up as a nature reserve in 1895, if I remember rightly, or 75, you know, a long, long time ago. And, and it has been a very long process to get it to where it is today. Mm-hmm. And, you know, we want the Public Service Commission actively thinking about that in thinking about how the public service needs to be set up, how they manage equipment processes for chief executives, how they manage chief executives. Can I, Can I just ask a specific one just on your recommendation to, to do with this?

I apologize for interrupting, but I'm just That's all right. We've only got a minute left. Um, it, it says that you want to retain the provision in section 11 in relation to supporting, you know, the long-term interest. But in this bill that provision is retained. It just adds a second section after about the chief executive. I, I, I just wonder, is there a, so, so we actually are keeping that section clause 11.

I'm not sure if that addresses your concern or is it adding the specific chief, chief executive stuff that's an issue. It's adding the specific chief executive stuff. So it's, it's, what does the chief executive focus on that that is our major concern, I guess, Right? How, how do, do you know how they manage it now?

'cause at the moment it doesn't say that the chief executive should manage it, focus on this either. I, I used to be the person who managed the chief executives relationship with the State Services Commission, but that was a long time ago before this act. Okay. Um, and in those days what we had were KPIs and Oh yeah.

And the chief executive was judged about how well they had progressed, and there was a conversation about the KPIs. Now, if that's the sort of thing that they're still doing, you know, that type of approach, what we want is to be sure that the Public Service Commission is having a lot of KPIs that are about that stewardship role about is the department gradually getting a stronger department and more able to deliver for the government of the day as opposed to simply delivering for the government of the day.

Thank you. And so, yeah, so that's our concern is how the chief executive, I, I appreciate that clarification. Thanks. Thanks for humoring me. We are actually out of our time there. But thank you very much, um, for joining us today and joining us in person. We appreciate it. We are now gonna move online, I believe, to the policy hub at Vicki Uni. Um, welcome. We've got until eight 20 for your submission.

Um, you might like to leave some time for questions. It's all yours. Thank you very much, chair and members of the committee for the opportunity to appear today. Because of the short amount of time you've written out the, what I wanna say in five minutes to give opportunity for questions. So please forgive me not giving eye contact to you as I go through this.

I want to begin by saying I fully support the attention being given to legislation that underpins the roles, responsibilities, and the ways of working of our public service. My comments today focus on one specific aspect of the bill, the role of the public service in long-term thinking and stewardship.

At present, the act recognizes that public servants should ensure advice is provided on the long-term implications of policies, not just immediate costs and benefits. The bill removes that recognition that may look like a small drafting change, but it has large consequences.

Why does this matter? It's easy to list some of the issues that will profoundly shape New Zealand's future. Artificial intelligence is already changing, uh, the way that we, uh, the nature of work and many aspects of society. The aging of our population will require a different shape of our health and care systems, and will have implications for who is working and how we afford the society we want.

The changing world order is raising questions of security and the future of democracy itself. And climate change will bring a range of challenges, and together these different forces will deliver a very different future for us. These are not short term issues. They demand careful consideration and consistency over decades, not a seesawing of shifting priorities every few years.

If we want to leave a stable and fair legacy for our mouna, we have to design our system to balance the urgent with the longer term challenges to think about tomorrow and not just today. The challenge is that the political imperative in democracies is to focus on the present and the term of government.

Former Prime Minister Jeffrey Palmer once said, the very structure of government decision making is geared to meet the needs of the present, not the future. We need to build in a system that pays attention to the longer term challenges. To balance this, under the current act, chief executives are required to provide advice on long-term implications of policy decisions.

Clause 27 of the Bill repeals that requirement from my own experience, which was 30 years in the public sector, including 10 years in senior policy roles and 25 years working internationally on foresight and future governance. I can tell you this, if long-term thinking is not clearly required, it will not happen.

Clause 45 removes the obligation of every department to produce long-term insight briefings instead just one a year by the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, I reviewed the first round of L tibs. They showed real promise. There were 19 briefings, some detailed analysis, some genuine pieces of foresight, and together they created a valuable picture of challenges and opportunities ahead for New Zealand.

However, I wouldn't recommend keeping what we have. Um, it was inefficient. It would be more efficient to start by doing a national horizon scan. Second, I do agree that there should be fewer L tibs not 19, maybe three or four per term. Third, there needs to be dedicated expertise.

You wouldn't go to the dentist and have a dentist remove a tooth the first time they've ever done the tooth. You want people with experience if they're looking at the future challenges and opportunities for New Zealand. And fourth, I would change it. So there's early engagement with ministers. My experience in foresight shows that unless you have the people with the power to act involved in foresight work, you'll just have a nice pretty piece of information at the end.

So we need to have engagement with ministers early on. The bill's proposal for DPMC to produce a central briefing aligns with the first step but not the rest. So I'd like to offer three recommendations to strengthen the bill. First, reinstate the expectation that departmental chief executives must ensure advice is provided on the long-term implications of policies.

That clause isn't symbolic. It's a statement that stewardship of the future is a core public service duty. Second, build on the DPMC re briefing by requiring three or four additional departmental l tibs to focus on key cross-cutting government issues that allows us to keep a good eye on future opportunities and challenges while using resources wisely.

And third, embed foresight capability in that work, because long-term thinking that's good requires skill, structure and practice. In conclusion, the Public Service Act should not only support good policy today, it should safeguard the ability of the public service to look beyond the political cycle cycle to successive governments.

So success, sorry. So successive governments make wise enduring decisions. Removing the explicit recognition of that role would be a backward step reinstating. It would send a clear message that the stewardship of New Zealand's future is not optional. It is part of what it means to serve the public. Thank you.

Thank you very much. Uh, I'll just see who's got questions around the table. No, they don't appear to be any, um, very grateful for your time and for your very, um, thorough and well set out written submission as well. We appreciate you joining us this morning. Um, we are gonna move on in the room. I think we have Dr.

Malcolm Menzies. Thank you. Come and take a seat please. Thank You. Um, and there is five minutes set aside for your submission, so I'll just leave you to it, but I'll let you know when there's a minute or so to run. Uh, Tina, thank you for this opportunity.

Uh, I think I'm gonna follow up theme of long-term thinking and, um, uh, a lot of what I have to say resonates with Andrew Jackson just said, but, but I have a slight difference of, of approach and emphasis. Um, the main point I want to make in my old submission is that the public service bill provides almost the last chance to retain a recognized government-wide futures thinking capability in R Tower or New Zealand.

Our country lags far behind others, including Australia, Singapore, Wales, Canada, Finland, the European Union, and the OECD, who have highly developed capabilities and strategic foresight. We have a history of failed attempts to embed futures thinking, going all the way back to the National Development Conferences of the 1960s, the Commission for the Future in the 1980s and the Knowledge Wave Conference in 2001.

The previous long-term insights briefings were set up to fail. And Andrew Jackson, Jonathan Boston and I wrote an article, uh, assessing that the ltip, which you may have or may not have seen, um, although many of the briefings were of high quality, and the people who produced them deserved praise, the briefings involved much duplication of work and inevitably produced a fragmented picture of what the future might hold.

There were 1919, uh, long-term insights briefings, uh, but only three were debated in parliament. That debate was both gratifying and disappointing, gratifying, and that it was held at all. Not so long ago to wish for such a futures oriented debate in Parliament would've been completely fanciful.

A few mps made a decent attempt to discuss long, long-term intergenerational issues. I thought Andy Foster was on this committee. He was one of the, the people who showed some, um, uh, intergenerational thinking, but there were no ministers present in the house, which was less than 20% full. I watched in pretty much splendid isolation from the gallery before I was shooed out in preparation for made speech.

It could hardly be said that the Al Alter process attracted a lot of political public attention. That's the end thing, uh, vast half full. I'm relieved that the long-term insights briefing concept has been retained in the proposed amendments to the act, and there is room for improvement. And my, my take on this is that in particular, the L tib needs to be given more prominence and more heft.

I know from experience of managing a triannual review of retirement income policies, that three years comes around too soon. That's just one specific policy area. People ask, haven't we just done this? Um, it would be better to produce a solid long-term insights briefing every five years to reflect the breadth and depth of the work required.

A five year cycle would also disconnect the briefings from political manage management and manipulation. I'm not naive though, and I understand that governments will not want to have, have their feet held to the fire over an ltip. The ltip will need to be pitched at a very high level, and its power will come through its transparency in the promotion of debate about options for building resilience.

The purpose of futures thinking is not to predict the future, but to identify key trends and uncertainties and probable possible and preferred futures and their implications for the present so that strategies, policies, and plans can be made for more made, more anticipatory acceptable, adaptable and robust in the future that eventually emerges.

To that end, it makes no sense to issue an alt unless public and private sector organizations consider how they will respond in their planning. This response need not be mandated, but it should be encouraged. In my written submission, I've suggested some wordings for consideration. There should also be a dedicated independent foresight futures, uh, unit within the DPMC, uh, to support the chief executive preparing each altar.

I dream of the 2030 altar being the subject of a substantial and serious futures debate in a full House of Representatives led off by the Prime Minister before a packed gallery. Thank you. Thank you. Um, we've just got time for one. We've got about 30 seconds. Francisco. Uh, thank you so much, Sharon. Thank you for your submission.

Um, just curious why the DPMC as opposed to something like the PSC? The PSC? Uh, well, it's a good question. I mean, it's just, it's what's in the act, so go with that. Okay. There needs to be some central agency. Yeah. Yeah. And DPMC does have a track record. It's, uh, it produced, uh, resources to support the Al Tips futures.

They've got a very good website, actually. It's good resources on it, so they've got some history. Okay. Yeah. Well, thank you, um, very much for joining us. We, we appreciate your time and your, um, also the, just the suggested change of wording is very specific, which is helpful. Thank you. Um, we're gonna move back online for our next submitter from appearance of Vision Impaired New Zealand.
Are they? Yep. They're New Zealand. We just give them a minute to turn on their camera.

And good morning, Dr. Graham. We have got, um, 10 minutes for your group. I'll let you know when there's a minute to run. Um, otherwise, the time is all yours. Oh, your, thank you so much for having us here today. Um, uh, you would've read, uh, written submission, and we just wanted to highlight a few things in our oral submission about the bill.

Uh, while we appreciate the intent of the bill, particularly around efficiency, stability, and accountability, which are all important things with the public service, uh, are organizations deeply concerned that several of the proposed changes, uh, risk undermining, inclusion, equity, and accessibility for disabled people and their families, uh, or in particular efficiency and accountability are important.

Uh, but when they come at the expense of participation, inclusion and fairness, they end up not being particularly efficient at all. Uh, the bill removes explicit references to diversity, equity, and inclusion from the Public Service Act. Uh, we acknowledge that these ideas appear in other legislation.

Um, however, removing them from the centrality of the Public Service Act does send a powerful signal, which is that inclusion of disabled people is optional, not essential, uh, for families like the ones we represent. This sends a signal that disability issues are easily sidelined and that their children have no place in the public service.

It also reduces the visibility of accessibility needs and requirements, reduces the visibility of things like reasonable accommodations and results in a less inclusive practice across government agencies and their ministries. A truly effective public service is one that serves its population, and that must include disabled children For our families, our disabled children will grow up and become adults.

And so we obviously take a very long-term thinking approach. And it's really important to be able to see visibility now at this stage so that our parents can see a future for their children as they grow into adulthood. Uh, we recognize that public resources must be used wisely, but when the type of efficiency that is prioritized is a very narrowly defined monetary only focus, this means that disabled children and their families and disabled adults end up losing out tailored supports.

Accessible systems and reasonable accommodations are not always able to be delivered as cheaply or as quickly as for non-disabled people. When performance is framed solely around a limited understanding of efficiency, this risks cutting the very supports that enable widespread participation, independence, and contributions by disabled people and their families.

This approach runs counter to New Zealand's obligations under the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. This convention requires that governments take active steps to include, not exclude disabled people from public life. The changes in this bill risk, uh, actively excluding disabled people from participating in public life.

I also want to draw the committee's attention to the employment outcome and the Refresh New Zealand disability strategy. Uh, at the time of submission, the strategy wasn't out yet, so I didn't include it so much in our mission submission. But one of the core outcomes of the New Zealand disability strategy is employment.

Uh, and the goal is that disabled people will have meaningful career opportunities equal to non-disabled people and be valued the same way disability Confident employers will recognize disabled people's talents and provide accessible and inclusive workplaces throughout the employment lifecycle.

The disability strategy then lays out practical actions to achieve this, such as improving pathways into employment, building employer confidence, supporting accessible workplaces, and promoting awareness of the value that disabled people as employees bring the proposed legislation undermines actively undermines this outcome of the New Zealand disability strategy.

Uh, the actions outlined on the disability strategy do depend somewhat on a public service that leads by example, one that models inclusion of disabled people in recruitment and career development and in workplace accessibility, a public service that understands what reasonable accommodations look like in practice and what is required to promote and include and make these an ordinary part of the service by removing explicit commitments to equity and inclusion.

This bill undermines the employment goals stated in the strategy and narrowly efficiency focused public service will not invest in the time flexibility, or reasonable accommodations that are required to recruit and retain disabled staff or to ensure accessible workplaces and digital systems.

Thus, risk reversing progress towards the strategies vision of a non disabling society. And it directly conflicts with the state of goal that disabled people will have meaningful career opportunities equal to non-disabled people For our families. Seeing disabled people employed in the public service gives them a hope and a vision that their child, while born blind, does not have limited opportunities in life.

That there are places for them to work and to live independently. And this gives them an ability to engage with the necessary services in the early intervention to make that long-term goal happen. Uh, the bill expands the powers of T six executives to delegate functions to contracted employees.

This shift risks weakening accountability for accessibility and inclusion. When services are outsourced, it is our families who end up facing inconsistency in quality, inconsistent accessibility, and inconsistent responsiveness. It becomes increasingly harder to identify who is responsible for a particular piece or, uh, service.

And this makes it easier for obligations towards disabled people, disabled children, and their families to fall through the cracks if contractors are not held to the same accessibility and inclusion standards as the public service itself. Again, it's our families, our disabled children, and disabled New Zealanders who are disadvantages.

Similarly, while we welcome the Bill's emphasis on digital leadership, and we are big fans of accessible technology without mandatory accessibility standards and universal design, digital systems continue to exclude our families. We've really noticed that, that if accessibility isn't mandatory, people drop it off.

Uh, public service must ensure that every New Zealand citizen can independently access information forms and online services. We've had to fight so hard already for a number of these to be accessible for our children. Uh, we are concerned that this changes in the school will drive those, uh, changes backwards over the long term.

Finally, amendments related to emergency and continuity planning could help improve protections for disabled people, but only if disability inclusion is made explicit. We have seen repeatedly in emergencies that when planning doesn't include disabled perspectives, uh, access to essential information, evacuation and supports is compromised.

This occurred in Christchurch where nobody knew who the blind and low vision persons are, and they weren't included when they needed to be evacuated. Mm-hmm. Uh, in closing, um, we would like to see that any changes to the public service, uh, strengthen New Zealand's commitment to disabled people, to accessibility, uh, and that our families will be able to rely on a public service that understands and meets their needs, not one that sidelines them in the name of efficiency.

A truly efficient and effective public service is one that enables every person, including those with disabilities to participate fully in education, employment, and community life. Thank you. And for our last minute, we've got, um, question from Laman. Lydia Sini. Um, thank you for your submission.

Really helpful to, um, clearly understand the effects, um, should the legislation go through. I have, my question is around the, um, equity inclusion and participation. You've highlighted a number of, um, if things are reversed and it's current form, can you just, um, really speak to the disability inclusion and so important in terms of not just children, but all the way to the workforce, why it's really critical that that part of, or a big part of our community needs to be, um, included in the public service?

Sure. So first off, it's a human right. So that's a really big reason. Like it is a basic human right, um, under the U-N-C-R-P-D for disabled people to be included equitably and similarly to non-disabled. Um, secondly, uh, disabled people exist. Uh, we are a huge part of the community.

The latest figures have one in six on New Zealanders having some form of disability. Now, obviously, the level of that, uh, ability varies, but, uh, if you're thinking about one in six New Zealanders, you need to have disability perspectives to understand what is required. Um, for us, blindness and low vision is a really particular disability, and if you don't understand what's required in terms of inclusion and access for that disability, you are not going to be able to meet it.

So it's super important that you are talking to people who understand what does accessibility look like for a blind person? What are the things that we need to implement at this level? What is an accessible document? If we put a document up online and a blind person can't access it and read it, that's a huge problem. So you need people in the public service who are able to do that for you, with you, and ensure that in terms of the public facing, that people who are public facing who are disabled can access that material. Um,

Thank you. We are, um, sadly, um, a bit over time there, but we appreciate it. We're gonna move on. I Could've gone on and on, I'm sure. Uh, next online submission is the a Asian Legal Network. I think we have Darren joining us. Welcome. We've got, uh, a 10 minutes set aside for you. Um, and I'll let you know when there's a minute to go. It's all yours.

Kiara, Tim, uh, Kiara. So we're Darren, uh, sine and Darren. So submitting on behalf of the Asian Legal Network, so an organization of lawyers, former judges, academics, and community advocates, and we strongly oppose the Public Service Amendment bill. So we take our written submission as read, and today we focus on three critical points, the repeal of diversity and inclusion provisions, the removal of pay equity protections that disproportionately affect Asian women, and the deletion of references to long-term public interest

and active citizenship from the acts purpose. So first on diversity and inclusion, one of the key drivers for removing inclusion and diversity is the focus, or is this focus on merit based appointments. So we believe it's a little bit offensive to suggest that meritocracy and diversity are somehow opposed, as if diverse candidates lack merit.

Uh, we believe there's categorically false meritocracy has demonstrably failed to achieve diversity in our public service. So, DEI may not be perfect solution, but it is an essential policy tool addressing substantial bias and lack of representation. Second, diversity simply makes better policy.

So if our public service reflects our communities, we get better insights, better consultation, and better outcomes. Diverse teams, we believe build trust in democratic institutions that we do see as weakening. And third representation creates ripples. So when people in our communities see themselves in public service, it facilitates civic engagement, creates belonging, and yet this bill removes the very language about facilitating active citizenship that enables this participation.

You cannot achieve the outcomes we expect from public service without diversity as part of the framework. Therefore, we urge you to reject this bill. So we recommend you remove Clause 35, which removes the requirement on chief executives and boards to promote diversity and inclusiveness amongst other provisions that roll back diversity down.

On the second point, on pay equity and removal of bias, we are quite concerned, uh, with the removal of the good employer provisions, um, under section, uh, 73, uh, three, um, subsections INJ, those provisions require the chief executives to include considering, um, pay equity between female male employees, as well as the importance of remuneration to be free, uh, from bias, not limited to gender bias.

These provisions are important for our communities. We have recently submitted to the, uh, people's Select Committee on Pay Equity. What we would, what we found in our, in our research is that Asian women get, are disproportionately affected by the ethnic gender wage gap. Uh, it is one of the few ethnic gender wage gaps that are actually increasing.

Uh, currently the gender wage gap sits at 14.6% on average, 7.6% for women, but up to 24.2% for Asian women, and 33% in, um, different in various sectors, including the academic health sector. We think that these statistics are very shocking, and the public service should be taking the lead in addressing pay equity.

It has flow on effects by viewing, um, increasing things like representation, trust in the public service, and it, and it supports mutually supports the points that, um, sine has made about diversity and inclusion more generally. It is an open secret that the public service doesn't reflect, um, and doesn't represent all of our communities at present.

It, um, doesn't do very well in terms of representation at the leadership level. And things like pay equity are some of the barriers. It's not a perfect solution, but we think that just on fairness, public, uh, or just on fairness, pay equity is important, and more importantly, it disproportionately affects, uh, w in our community.

And so we think that the removal of references to pay equity, particularly in the good employer, uh, provisions, are deeply concerning. And we urge this committee not to recommend, um, these provisions, uh, to continue. We also wanna make a short comment on the purpose of the bill. Interestingly, the current purpose, uh, already states the importance of the long term public interest to be considered, and also that it is on the government to support and facilitate active citizenship.

Find it quite curious that the amended purpose does not make reference to these, um, two points in particular, long term public, um, interest in active citizenship as well as a, a turn instead to a focus on, quote unquote meet the needs of New Zealanders. At present, we think that the long term view that the current purpose states is in the benefit of Asian communities, and particularly the focus on active ci, active citizenship.

The long-term view is important because we know that AL is changing, it's changing demographically. There are, uh, changes on the horizon, um, that the public interest needs to consider and to take into account. And we think that this purpose in which it frames the whole act is something that, that, um, shouldn't be removed.

We also know that active citizenship is particularly important, uh, for Asian communities. We've just recently made, uh, our own submission on the, um, electoral amendment bill, and we have, we made concern. We, we set out our concerns there about how Asian communities have been historically and con and contemporarily, um, excluded, or that there are barriers to engaging in the democratic process, active citizenship, and facilitating, facilitating that should be part of what the public service does.

We think that there are long term benefits to this, such as strengthening democracy in a time like 2025 when we see that trust in democratic institutions are rolling backwards globally. When we step back, all these three, three points that we've highlighted, inclusion and diversity, pay equity, and more broadly in terms of the purpose, uh, we don't think that that reflects what the public service should be heading towards.

And in fact, it doesn't set up alor well to, um, address future concerns. Before we continue and, and wrap up, I just wanted to turn over to the committee and to engage any questions that, uh, the committee has for us. Yeah, I'll start with, uh, Francisco Hernandez. Uh, thank you so much for that chair, and thank you for your submission, uh, Asian legal Network.

Um, can you elaborate on how having, um, DEI could actually enhance merit based appointments rather than going against it? Uh, yeah. So for example, uh, say for example in, we'll take counsel, so real local, uh, example.
